planting.note: Divide established clumps in spring just as growth resumes, or sow seed in fall for natural cold stratification and spring germination; obedient plant spreads aggressively by rhizomes and self-seeding in moist, fertile soils — divide every 2–3 years to manage spread and maintain vigor, and consider planting in lean, drier soils to limit rhizome spread.

directions: Plant in average to moist, well-drained to moderately wet soil in full sun to partial shade; obedient plant is one of the most distinctive late-summer native wildflowers of eastern and central North America, producing stiff, upright square stems topped with dense, tapering spikes of tubular, two-lipped flowers in bright pink to magenta-purple from July through September, at a time when many other wildflowers are past their peak. The common name 'obedient plant' refers to the curious mechanical property of the individual flowers: each floret is hinged on its calyx and will stay precisely where it is manually repositioned on the spike, whether left, right, up, or down — a botanical party trick that has delighted gardeners for centuries and makes it instantly memorable. It is an important late-season nectar plant for ruby-throated hummingbirds, bumblebees, and various long-tongued native bees; the square stems and opposite leaves identify it as a member of the mint family (Lamiaceae), and like many mints it spreads vigorously by creeping rhizomes and can colonize large areas rapidly in moist, fertile conditions. Plant in lean, slightly drier soils to rein in the rhizome spread, or divide and share clumps every 2–3 years; named cultivars like 'Miss Manners' (white, less spreading) are more garden-well-behaved but the straight species has greater wildlife value.

planting.note: Divide established clumps in spring or fall, or direct sow seed in fall for natural cold stratification; clustered mountain mint spreads moderately by rhizomes and self-seeds in average to moist soils — it is less aggressively spreading than some Pycnanthemum species and is generally well-behaved in garden settings while still forming useful colonies over time.

directions: Plant in average to moist, well-drained soil in full sun to partial shade; clustered mountain mint is widely regarded as the most ornamentally distinguished of all native Pycnanthemum species, set apart from the more common P. tenuifolium and P. virginianum by its large, broad, silvery-white bracts that surround the dense, clustered flower heads and persist long after the small white flowers fade — from midsummer onward the upper portions of the plant take on a striking silver-white frosted appearance that is exceptionally showy and provides weeks of ornamental interest. It is one of the single most valuable native plants for pollinators in eastern North America: studies at the Lady Bird Johnson Wildflower Center have recorded over 60 species of native bees visiting a single planting, along with dozens of butterfly species, making it a keystone resource in any pollinator garden or native meadow planting. The foliage is strongly mint-scented when crushed and deer-resistant; the plant spreads moderately by rhizomes to form a useful, weed-suppressing colony in average to moist garden soils and tolerates partial shade better than most other mountain mints. Clustered mountain mint is a premier native plant choice for rain gardens, stream edges, moist meadow margins, and any garden situation with consistently adequate moisture and partial to full sun.

planting.note: Direct sow in fall in lean, well-drained, sandy to loamy soil in full sun; Maximilian sunflower cold-stratifies naturally over winter and germinates reliably in spring — it can also be spring-sown after the last frost but fall sowing typically produces more vigorous first-year plants; avoid rich, moist soils that encourage excessive vegetative growth at the expense of flowering and increase susceptibility to lodging.

directions: Direct sow in fall or early spring in lean, well-drained, dry to average soil in full sun; Maximilian sunflower is the great giant of North American native perennial sunflowers, a towering prairie and Great Plains species that typically reaches 5–10 feet in height and produces hundreds of bright yellow, 2–3 inch daisy flowers in long, arching, leafy-bracted racemes from late September into October — its late bloom season is its defining ecological asset, providing abundant nectar, pollen, and seed when most other wildflowers have finished for the year. Named by German botanist Adelbert von Chamisso in honor of Prince Maximilian of Wied-Neuwied, who documented the species during his famous 1832–34 expedition into the Great Plains, this sunflower is native to the central prairies and plains from Canada through Texas and is one of the most important late-season food plants for migrating monarch butterflies, native bees, and the many sparrows and finches that consume its oil-rich seeds. It spreads by rhizomes to form large, vigorous colonies and can be assertive in rich, moist soils — plant in lean, dry ground to keep it better proportioned and contained; even in good soils it may require staking or the companionship of sturdy native grasses to remain upright. Maximilian sunflower is an indispensable component of tall-grass prairie restorations and naturalistic meadow plantings, providing essential fall resources at a critical time in the migration and overwintering preparation of numerous wildlife species.

planting.note: Direct sow in fall in lean, well-drained, dry to average soil in full sun; willowleaf sunflower cold-stratifies naturally over winter and germinates reliably in spring; like Maximilian sunflower it benefits from lean, drier soils that keep stems upright and plants better proportioned — rich, moist soils encourage excessive vegetative growth and floppy stems.

directions: Direct sow in fall in lean, very well-drained, dry to average soil in full sun; willowleaf sunflower is one of the most architecturally distinctive native perennial sunflowers in North America, immediately recognizable by its exceptionally long, narrow, willow-like leaves (4–8 inches long, less than half an inch wide) that clothe the stem in dense, drooping, grass-like foliage from base to tip, creating a feathery, almost bamboo-like texture that is unlike any other sunflower species. From September through October the stems are topped with clusters of bright yellow, 2–3 inch daisy flowers with yellow-brown disk centers, providing the same important late-season food resources as Maximilian sunflower for pollinators and seed-eating birds at a critical time when most wildflowers have finished. Native to the rocky, dry prairies, limestone glades, and rocky hillsides of the south-central United States from Nebraska to Texas and Louisiana, it is naturally adapted to thin, alkaline, rocky soils and extreme drought — it performs best in lean, dry conditions that emphasize its graceful texture rather than producing excessive, floppy vegetative growth. Willowleaf sunflower spreads by rhizomes into handsome colonies and is an excellent structural plant for the back of a sunny native border, tall prairie planting, or naturalistic meadow where its fine-textured foliage provides visual interest from spring through frost.

planting.note: Direct sow in fall after scarifying the hard seed coat with sandpaper; yellow wild indigo requires natural cold stratification over winter and is slow from seed — expect 2–3 years to first bloom — but forms a long-lived, deep-rooted plant that resents disturbance once established; sow in a permanent location in lean to average, well-drained, acidic to neutral soil in full sun.

directions: Sow direct in fall after scarifying the hard seed coat, in lean to average, well-drained, sandy to loamy, acidic to neutral soil in full sun; yellow wild indigo is a nitrogen-fixing legume native to the Gulf Coast states and south-central United States that develops a deep, woody root system making transplanting inadvisable — establish from seed in a permanent location and cold-stratify naturally over winter. Baptisia sphaerocarpa is the yellow-flowered counterpart to the more widely grown blue wild indigo (B. australis), distinguished by its bright golden-yellow, pea-like flowers held in upright racemes from April through May — it typically blooms earlier in spring than blue indigo and is one of the finest yellow-flowered native perennials for hot, dry, sunny conditions in the South. Like all Baptisia species it is extraordinarily long-lived and slow to establish from seed, requiring 2–3 years before first bloom and reaching full maturity and maximum ornamental effect after 5 or more years; once established it is essentially indestructible, surviving extreme drought, poor soils, and neglect. The inflated, rounded seed pods (sphaerocarpa = round-fruited) are ornamentally attractive in fall, and the blue-green, trifoliate leaves provide handsome fine-textured foliage from spring through frost; it is an important early-season nectar and pollen plant for queen bumblebees and specialist native bees before most other prairie wildflowers have opened.
sources: North Carolina Extension Gardener Plant Toolbox

planting.note: Direct sow in fall after scarifying the hard seed coat with sandpaper or nicking with a knife; Nuttall's wild indigo requires cold stratification over winter and is slow from seed — expect 2–3 years to first bloom — but once established with its deep taproot it is extremely drought-tolerant and essentially permanent; sow in a permanent, well-drained location in lean to sandy soil in full sun.

directions: Sow direct in fall after scarifying the hard seed coat, in lean, very well-drained, sandy or rocky, acidic to neutral soil in full sun; Nuttall's wild indigo is a compact, small-statured Baptisia native to the sandy pine woodlands, dry prairies, and open disturbed ground of Texas, Louisiana, and surrounding Gulf Coast states, where it is one of the more drought-tolerant members of the genus — well suited to the thin, acidic, sandy soils of the coastal plain. Named in honor of Thomas Nuttall, the English-American botanist who collected extensively across North America in the early nineteenth century, this species produces small, white to cream-colored, pea-like flowers in slender racemes in spring, followed by the inflated seed pods characteristic of the genus; it is smaller and more delicate in appearance than B. australis or B. sphaerocarpa, making it suitable for scale-appropriate plantings in smaller native gardens. Like all wild indigos it is a nitrogen-fixing legume, develops a deep taproot that makes transplanting impractical, and is very slow to reach first bloom from seed — patience of 2–3 years is required; in return it becomes an essentially permanent, zero-maintenance plant that thrives in the lean, dry conditions that challenge most ornamentals. Nuttall's wild indigo is an important early-season resource for bumblebee queens and specialist native bees in the Southeast and is a valuable addition to native plantings in the Gulf Coast region.
